choices: Minimum-variance portfolios for each given expected return level
questions.explanation: The frontier traces the minimum risk achievable at each expected return. Rational mean-variance investors hold the upper half (above the minimum-variance point).

questions.prompt: The tangent portfolio is special because:
choices: It contains only the risk-free asset
Among all risky portfolios on the efficient frontier, it has the highest Sharpe ratio (best risk-return trade-off)
questions.explanation: The tangent portfolio is where a line from R_f touches (is tangent to) the efficient frontier. That tangency point is the highest-Sharpe risky portfolio.

choices: Every investor's optimal allocation is a combination of the risk-free asset and the tangent (market) portfolio
Every investor should hold exactly two funds named A and B
The market should be divided into long-only and long-short funds
Bond and stock allocations must be 50/50
questions.explanation: Two funds (risk-free + tangent) suffice for every investor. The proportion of each varies with risk tolerance, but the risky portfolio is always the same.

questions.prompt: Under what conditions does mean-variance analysis give exactly correct portfolio recommendations?
choices: Returns are heavily skewed with fat tails
questions.explanation: Normal returns OR quadratic utility makes mean-variance fully sufficient. Fat tails and skewness violate the framework's assumptions; market efficiency is unrelated to this question.

furtherReading.title: Ruminating on Asset Allocation
furtherReading.why: Marks directly addresses how endowments and pensions blend ownership and debt assets along the efficient frontier — real-world optimal-portfolio-choice debate.
